title: The Candidate

jokes.text: Twyla congratulates Moira on running for town council — something Moira has no idea about
jokes.setup: Twyla offers congratulations to Moira, who assumes it must be for something she knows about
jokes.punchline: It's for running for town council — news to Moira entirely
jokes.explanation: A clean misdirection that sets the A-plot in motion. Moira's baffled 'What, excuse me?' is perfectly in character. The joke works because Twyla's cheerful cluelessness is already an established comedic engine, and Moira's imperious confusion is a great foil.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, you know what they say: never assume, because it makes an ass out of you and... me, I suppose, in this instance. Though I prefer the French version: 'assumer' — it's far more elegant.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Well, you could say something, think of it as a clue.'
jokes.setup: Twyla hesitates and asks if she should say anything
jokes.punchline: Moira imperiously instructs her to speak, framing it as a clue — completely missing that SHE is the one who should already know
jokes.explanation: Pure Moira character comedy — her regal, game-show-host framing of being told about her own supposed announcement is delightfully self-unaware. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Absolutely not. I am not going to sit around a table with a bunch of small-town bureaucrats and pretend to care about zoning permits and septic tank regulations. I have a reputation to maintain.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Never assume, dear. It makes an ass out of both of us.'
jokes.setup: Twyla apologizes for misreading the situation
jokes.punchline: Moira delivers the classic 'assume' aphorism with full theatrical gravitas, calling Twyla an ass to her face in the politest possible way
jokes.explanation: An old chestnut ('assume makes an ass of u and me') but Moira's delivery and oblivious condescension elevate it. The originality score reflects it's a borrowed phrase; the character integration score reflects how perfectly it fits her patrician lecturing mode.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: This town is so... so...

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Mom, don't.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: ...quaint.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh thank God.

jokes.text: John suggests Moira running for council would give her an outlet to channel all that 'passion' — with a very loaded pause before 'passion'
jokes.setup: John diplomatically floats the idea of Moira using council as an outlet for her... energy
jokes.punchline: The word 'passion' is delivered with clearly ironic quotation marks — implying something more like 'chaotic, destructive energy'
jokes.explanation: John's gentle, long-suffering diplomacy is one of the show's great recurring pleasures. The pause-before-'passion' is a perfectly calibrated bit of husbandly self-preservation. High character integration because this is quintessential John Rose — loving but exhausted.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: But Mrs. Rose, you'd be so good at it. You're so organized, and you have great ideas about things.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Plus, my tarot cards said that someone close to me would be in a position of power soon.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: I just assumed it was you.

jokes.text: Moira: 'I'd sooner poke my eyes out with hat pins.'
jokes.setup: John suggests council might be a good outlet
jokes.punchline: Moira's spectacularly over-the-top rejection of the idea using a Victorian torture image
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly economical. 'I'd sooner poke my eyes out with hat pins' is perfectly Moira — archaic, melodramatic, specific. The specificity of 'hat pins' over 'needles' or 'sticks' is the kind of detail that makes Moira's voice so distinctive. High quotability, strong craft.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What is this? What is all of this?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, are those my clothes?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Your clothes are under my bed? Under MY bed, Alexis. Do you understand what you've done? Do you have any idea what this means?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That I needed storage?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It means that my entire life has been a lie. That everything I thought I knew about my personal space, about boundaries, about the sanctity of my own bedroom — it's all been compromised. I'm going to need to sit down.

jokes.text: John: 'Well, that's subtle.' — after Moira's hat pin declaration
jokes.setup: Moira announces she'd rather gouge her eyes out than run for council
jokes.punchline: John's perfectly dry 'Well, that's subtle' undercuts her drama with maximum calm
jokes.explanation: Three words of maximum impact. John's dry wit as Moira's long-suffering straight-man is perfectly executed here. The economy score is nearly perfect — there is no shorter way to achieve the same comic effect.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, there are wet towels on the bedroom floor. Again. Do you know what happens to hardwood when it's exposed to moisture? It warps. It buckles. It essentially gives up on life.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And these shoes—these are Louboutins, are they not? Yes. They're worth more than our first house, and you've just... abandoned them here like some sort of sad, red-soled monument to your complete lack of regard for anything.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You know what? I don't even care about the shoes anymore. I care about what this says about you as a person. About us as a family. About the trajectory of Western civilization.

jokes.text: Moira struggling to find the word to describe the town, going around and around — 'somewhere less... less what? What's the word I'm trying to think of?' — and everyone hoping she doesn't find it
jokes.setup: Moira attempts to say the town is beneath her, searching for a polite word
jokes.punchline: John: 'Well, let's hope we don't find it, okay?' — everyone dreading the inevitable insult, interrupted mercifully by Twyla
jokes.explanation: A sustained cringe-comedy bit where the comedy IS the anticipation. John actively prays she doesn't land on a word — the audience knows exactly what word Moira is circling. The scene milks the discomfort beautifully, and Twyla's oblivious intervention is a perfect release valve. Strong structure and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why are all your clothes wet?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh, I was at the spa and I may have accidentally fallen into the hot tub.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: May have?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, I definitely fell in. But David, I'm not entirely sure it was water.

jokes.text: Twyla cheerfully asks 'Are we ready to order, councilor?' — immediately after Moira said she'd sooner gouge her eyes out than run
jokes.setup: The whole painful conversation about Moira absolutely NOT running for council
jokes.punchline: Twyla, completely unaware of the social dynamics, calls Moira 'councilor' anyway
jokes.explanation: A beautiful button on the scene — Twyla's cheerful obliviousness persists right through the awkwardness. Calling Moira 'councilor' after the whole conversation is a perfect callback and an efficient character joke about Twyla. Rewatch bonus for how it recontextualizes the whole opening.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, so you don't know anything about Twyla? At all?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No, like, we work together, but I don't really know her.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Oh my God, okay. She's a serial killer. Twyla's a serial killer.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What? No, she's not.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, you just said you don't know anything about her! That's exactly what someone would say if their coworker was a serial killer!

jokes.text: David's shared room is a disaster — wet clothing, clothes crammed under the bed, general squalor
jokes.setup: The Rose children, former billionaires, are sharing a tiny motel room
jokes.punchline: The physical reality of their living situation — wet mystery clothing, no storage — is the punchline to their entire rich-kid-falls-from-grace arc
jokes.explanation: A running visual gag throughout the series — the squalor of their motel room as a fish-out-of-water joke. Works steadily but isn't exceptional on its own; its comedy comes from accumulated context.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, Twyla and I have tea together every single day. That's what best friends do.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, you pay her to work here.

jokes.text: 'Look at your things. Look at your life!'
jokes.setup: David has found Alexis's clothes crammed under his bed
jokes.punchline: David's reaction escalates from practical complaint to existential dramatic indictment in two short sentences
jokes.explanation: Highly quotable. The pivot from the mundane ('your things') to the sweeping ('your life') in the space of four words is genuinely funny and very David. The parallel structure is clean and the escalation from complaint to condemnation is perfectly sized. One of the better lines in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not judging.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, your face is literally doing a judgement.

jokes.text: David: 'Why is this wet? Ewww!' / Alexis admits it might be her water she left on the floor
jokes.setup: David discovers something wet among his things
jokes.punchline: Alexis casually admits she kicked over her own glass of water onto his things, then deflects with 'what difference does it make?'
jokes.explanation: Classic sibling cringe comedy — Alexis's 'what difference does it make?' is peak narcissistic obliviousness. Functional but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Ray's seat is up for grabs.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I heard what you said.

jokes.text: Alexis announces she's having a 'fun little sleepover' with Twyla
jokes.setup: Alexis kicks David out of the room for the evening
jokes.punchline: The guest is Twyla — an unexpected choice that David immediately interrogates
jokes.explanation: Sets up the Alexis/Twyla friendship storyline. The comedy is in the incongruity of Alexis Rose — former socialite — having a sleepover with the cafe waitress. Solid but serves more as setup than payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We'll be working very closely together. In fact, I'd say we'll be practically joined at the hip.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, that's... that's the idea, yes.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Two men, one space, sharing everything. It'll be like we're married.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I... okay.

jokes.text: David: 'She could be a serial killer.' / Alexis: 'Twy and I have tea together like, every day, David.'
jokes.setup: David questions whether Alexis really knows Twyla
jokes.punchline: David's serial killer paranoia vs. Alexis's casual dismissal, which David then deflates: 'That she serves you... at the cafe'
jokes.explanation: A three-beat exchange with a great structure: paranoia → casual rebuff → devastating clarification. 'That she serves you... at the cafe' is a beautifully economical deflation of Alexis's class-oblivious claim to friendship. Strong character integration — David's suspicion and Alexis's obliviousness are both perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I think that's... that's wonderful. I'm very happy for you.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: And I should probably get going. I have a very important... thing.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Goodbye.

jokes.text: David's perfectly timed beat after 'Twy and I have tea together every day' — pausing, then: 'That she serves you... at the cafe.'
jokes.setup: Alexis claims she and Twyla are close friends
jokes.punchline: David's devastating two-beat clarification that the 'friendship' is just a service transaction
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the scene and one of the episode's standouts. The ellipsis before '...at the cafe' is doing enormous structural work — the pause lets the audience anticipate the deflation before it lands. It skewers Alexis's class blindness in seven words. The specificity ('at the cafe' rather than just 'she's your server') makes it especially surgical. High quotability, strong rewatch value.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, so you're the reason Mutt and I broke up?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Well, he came into the café one day and he was really upset about you moving to the city, and I just... I listened to him. And we talked. And then we started seeing each other.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Twyla! You can't just date someone right after they break up with me!

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: I know, I'm sorry. But we're actually really happy together now.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

jokes.text: David's escalating sarcasm about where Twyla will sleep, using the bedroom question as a passive-aggressive eviction complaint
jokes.setup: David is being kicked out of his own room for the night
jokes.punchline: He frames his objection as innocent concern about Twyla's sleeping arrangements, escalating to theatrical outrage
jokes.explanation: David's passive-aggression is well-deployed here. The comedy is in his theatrical indirection — pretending to worry about Twyla as a cover for his real complaint. Good character integration but slightly drawn out.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: The irony of this advice coming from someone desperately trying to avoid being alone

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Stop doing that with your face!' / David: silent Mhmm face
jokes.setup: David is making a withering face at Alexis during her non-answer
jokes.punchline: Alexis's exasperated demand that he stop 'doing that with his face' — and his knowing, unrepentant 'Hmm'
jokes.explanation: Daniel Levy's physical comedy — the withering facial expressions — is a signature of the show. 'Stop doing that with your face' is a perfectly quotable line that captures the sibling dynamic. High character integration. Rewatch bonus because the face IS the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm totally fine being alone. I don't need anyone.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Twyla! Wait! Twyla, come back!

jokes.text: Bob: 'His seat is up for grabs? I could say something pretty inappropriate right now.'
jokes.setup: John asks about the open council seat using the phrase 'up for grabs'
jokes.punchline: Bob flags the double entendre with schoolboy glee, then repeats it when John says 'filled'
jokes.explanation: Functional lowbrow humor — flagging a double entendre is a well-worn gag. What saves it is that it's doubled (first 'up for grabs,' then 'filled'), and Bob's character — the cheerfully crude mechanic — makes it feel earned. Not sophisticated, but it fits Bob perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, my hemorrhoids have been acting up again, so I'll have to call you back.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, I didn't need to know that.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh, come on, Johnny. We're friends. You can't keep these things bottled up inside.

jokes.text: Bob: 'It might be a nice change of pace from banging your head against the wall here every day.'
jokes.setup: Bob is ostensibly supporting John running for council
jokes.punchline: He frames it as relief from John's daily futility at the garage — a backhanded endorsement
jokes.explanation: A gentle dig at John's ineffectiveness at the garage. Works as mild irony but doesn't land hard enough to be exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'd like to get some nomination forms.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh, nomination forms? Well, that's great, Johnny. That's really great. You know, I think you've got a real shot at this thing.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: No, not at all. But I admire the confidence.

jokes.text: Bob: 'If I didn't know any better, I might think you had a crush on me!' — after realizing they'd work together on council AND at the garage
jokes.setup: Bob realizes John wanting his support means they'd be together at work AND on council
jokes.punchline: Bob's jokey suggestion that John has a crush on him, followed by spiraling reassurances that make it worse
jokes.explanation: A fun comedic beat that escalates nicely into Bob digging himself deeper with reassurances. The joke lives in Bob's complete lack of self-awareness about how his 'reassurances' sound.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: The second largest? Well, that just means you're the first to lose.

jokes.text: Bob: 'I don't mean to embarrass you.' / John: 'I'm not embarrassed!' / Bob: 'You have no reason to be embarrassed.' — spiraling
jokes.setup: Bob's 'crush' joke has made things deeply awkward
jokes.punchline: Bob keeps adding reassurances that each make John sound MORE embarrassed, culminating in 'you're a happily married man, and so am I!'
jokes.explanation: Classic escalation comedy — each reassurance makes the situation worse. 'You're a happily married man. And so am I!' is a perfect button. The structure is solid: Bob digs the hole, then keeps digging.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: In a town this size, there is no privacy in politics. Or anywhere else, for that matter.

jokes.text: John: 'Oh my. Okay, gotta go, Bob.' — fleeing the escalating awkwardness
jokes.setup: Bob has now loudly established that they're both happily married and John has no reason to be embarrassed about his crush
jokes.punchline: John's quiet, defeated 'Oh my. Okay, gotta go, Bob.' as he extricates himself
jokes.explanation: Eugene Levy's deadpan is perfectly suited to this exit. 'Oh my' is doing a lot of work — it's bewilderment, defeat, and dignity all at once. Great economy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, that's very sweet, Roland. Although, I have to say, taking your son for ice cream doesn't really qualify as parenting. That's just... bribery with dairy.

jokes.text: Alexis, trying to get Twyla for a sleepover, is told: 'part of me is a little relieved. Since you did tell me to break up with him, and then you dated him...'
jokes.setup: Alexis expresses sympathy for Twyla's breakup with Mutt
jokes.punchline: Twyla gently points out the obvious conflict of interest — Alexis told her to break up with him, then dated him herself
jokes.explanation: Twyla's sweet, non-confrontational delivery of a genuinely damning observation is perfectly calibrated. The joke is in the gap between what Twyla is actually saying and how blandly she says it. Good character work for both characters.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Is that your signature?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's my signature.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's just a line.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, a line's a signature.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'That's so sweet of you, but I'm gonna be fine.' — responding to Twyla pointing out Alexis wronged her
jokes.setup: Twyla just noted that Alexis told her to break up with Mutt and then dated him
jokes.punchline: Alexis completely misreads this as Twyla comforting HER — 'That's so sweet of you, but I'm gonna be fine' — as if she's the victim
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. Alexis's narcissistic inversion — receiving what should be an accusation as an expression of concern for HER — is executed with perfect timing and perfect character specificity. Only Alexis Rose would respond this way. The joke requires no set-up beyond what the show has built: we know exactly who Alexis is, and this confirms it. High craft, high impact, very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So what's your plan to get laid?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I'm thinking someone who's attractive, but not so attractive that they know they're attractive. Someone with a good job, but not so good that they don't have time for me. And ideally someone who's never seen me in a bad light, which means I basically have to find someone who's never seen me.

jokes.text: Alexis coaching Twyla on alone time while simultaneously trying to schedule a sleepover with her — giving advice she does not intend to follow
jokes.setup: Alexis passionately advocates for the importance of alone time after a breakup
jokes.punchline: Every sentence is undercut by her immediate attempts to get Twyla to come over and hang out with her
jokes.explanation: A sustained irony — Alexis preaching the gospel of alone time while desperately trying to avoid being alone herself. The joke accretes over several exchanges and gets funnier as it becomes more obvious she's talking about herself. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: People would think we're together.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, that's a fate worse than death.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh please, you'd be so lucky.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Lucky? I'd rather date a mannequin.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: At least a mannequin has better taste in clothes.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Says the woman who wore a swimsuit to a funeral.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'But call me if you happen to get off work early.' — immediately after her sermon on alone time
jokes.setup: Alexis has just given an extended speech about how important alone time is after a breakup
jokes.punchline: She immediately undercuts it with 'But call me if you happen to get off work early' — completely oblivious to the contradiction
jokes.explanation: A perfect button on the alone-time irony bit. The brevity and timing make it land hard — the gap between the sermon and the immediate contradiction is the joke. Alexis's self-unawareness is at its funniest here.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: There's been a rumor going around about me.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my God, Johnny. It's 2020. Who cares if you're gay?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm not gay. It's about the town council.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, that's much worse.

jokes.text: Alexis calling after Twyla: 'Twy? Call me if you get off work early!' — repeating the request louder across the cafe
jokes.setup: Alexis's sotto voce callback request was already funny
jokes.punchline: She doubles down by shouting it across the cafe, fully committed to both her hypocrisy and her loneliness
jokes.explanation: The escalation from private request to shouting across the room adds another layer to the joke. Alexis's total lack of self-consciousness about broadcasting her loneliness is perfectly in character and gets a good laugh.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I think what's happened here is you've suffered a minor stroke.

jokes.text: Roland: 'I just had to reschedule my colonoscopy.'
jokes.setup: Roland is on the phone doing something important-sounding as John arrives
jokes.punchline: The reveal that the 'important' call was to reschedule a colonoscopy — delivered with total casualness as though it's entirely normal business conversation
jokes.explanation: A quick, efficient Roland gag — his complete lack of decorum is well-established and this is a solid deployment of it. The 'colonoscopy' specificity makes it funnier than if he'd said 'doctor's appointment.'

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Working with the town council is like trying to apply winged eyeliner in a hurricane while someone's actively criticizing your technique.

jokes.text: Roland: 'It takes a brave man to face defeat yet again, when his ego is so battered and bruised.'
jokes.setup: John tells Roland he's picking up a nomination form for council
jokes.punchline: Roland immediately frames it as inevitable defeat — 'I gotta hand it to you' is a compliment that is immediately revealed as a backhanded insult
jokes.explanation: A solid Roland moment — his cheerful, oblivious condescension toward John is always a reliable comic dynamic. The 'battered and bruised ego' framing is specific enough to feel fresh.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Are you waiting for someone?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No, I just wanted some alone time.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You're sitting at the bar.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Alone time where people can see me.

jokes.text: Roland: 'So you're used to coming in second, huh?' — after John mentions running 'the second largest' video chain
jokes.setup: John cites his experience running 'the second largest retail video chain in North America'
jokes.punchline: Roland immediately weaponizes 'second' — 'Well, that's good to hear, so you're used to coming in second, huh?'
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's standout joke. The setup is elegant — John inadvertently gives Roland the perfect material by specifying 'second largest.' Roland's instantaneous pivot on 'second' is perfectly structured misdirection: what sounds like validation ('Well, that's good to hear') immediately becomes a devastating put-down. It's quotable, it's fast, it's structurally perfect, and it's character-specific (Roland's cheerful cruelty and John's pomposity colliding). Comparable to the 8.0 anchor example.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, Stevie is a really strong, independent woman. She doesn't need a man to validate her.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Unlike some people.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh, please. I dated you for like three weeks.

jokes.text: Roland's speech about political life: 'You walk into a restaurant, everybody's gonna know who you are!' / John: 'Well, everyone knows everyone here, because there's only one restaurant.'
jokes.setup: Roland warns that political life means loss of privacy, using restaurant fame as an example
jokes.punchline: John deflates the warning with the simple reality of small-town life — everyone already knows everyone, there's only one restaurant
jokes.explanation: A sharp observational joke that perfectly captures the absurdity of applying big-city political wisdom to a tiny town. The joke is in the logical precision of John's rebuttal — 'because there's only one restaurant' is a beautifully specific detail. Good structure, good character integration (John's pragmatism vs. Roland's pomposity).

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, he was.

jokes.text: Roland: 'I mean, just taking your boy for an ice cream cone can turn into a media circus.' / John: 'Well, your son is 30, and you never see him.'
jokes.setup: Roland warns about the sacrifice of family life in politics, citing taking 'your boy for ice cream'
jokes.punchline: John quietly devastates him: his 'boy' is 30 and estranged
jokes.explanation: John's quiet devastation of Roland's hypothetical is one of the best John Rose moments in the episode. It's dark — the son is estranged — but delivered with the same calm precision as the restaurant joke. The juxtaposition between Roland's earnest political wisdom and the sad reality of his family life is perfectly pitched.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm not antisocial, I'm just... selective about who I spend my time with.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Right, but like, you don't spend time with anyone.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Exactly. I'm very selective.

jokes.text: Roland signs 'under Date' on John's nomination form — John points it out, Roland: 'Yeah, like that's going to make a difference.'
jokes.setup: Roland offers to give John his first signature on the nomination form
jokes.punchline: He signs under 'Date' — and when John points it out, Roland dismisses it as if one misplaced signature won't matter anyway
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed visual/dialogue gag. The first laugh is the misfiled signature; the second is Roland's breezy dismissal implying John is so unelectable that the signature placement is the least of his problems. Two laughs from one beat — efficient writing.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I know winning by default isn't exactly exciting, but I'm still very enthusiastic about it.

jokes.text: Roland: 'Rrrrrr! Come're, you! I'm thirsty!' — after the whole exchange
jokes.setup: John has just left, the political conversation is over
jokes.punchline: Roland makes a random, animal-like sound of satisfaction and announces his thirst — a completely unearned non-sequitur
jokes.explanation: Roland's inexplicable post-conversation sound effect is pure Roland character comedy. It doesn't have much structure but it's a good button — the growl of satisfaction after verbally demolishing John. Absurdist in the best small-town-character way.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Moira, honey, I love you, but the town doesn't really know you. You've been here for what, five years? And most people still think you're a little... strange.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Strange? I'm eccentric. There's a difference.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Right, well, most of the town just calls it 'that woman who wears the big wigs.'

jokes.text: Stevie's flat 'Thrilling, can't you tell?' in response to being asked about her afternoon
jokes.setup: John/another character asks if Stevie had an exciting afternoon
jokes.punchline: Stevie's reply drips with boredom — 'Thrilling, can't you tell?' — entirely without affect
jokes.explanation: Quintessential Stevie deadpan. The 'can't you tell?' tag is the right flourish — she doesn't just negate the premise but invites you to observe her state for yourself. Economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I think it's wonderful that you're encouraging more people to run. It's very... charitable of you. After all, not everyone can be naturally gifted at it. Some people just need the extra motivation, you know? Though I suppose if we're being honest, some of those people probably shouldn't be running in public. But that's very altruistic of you to give them false hope.

jokes.text: Stevie interrogation of David about his plans for the evening — 'With what? With who? Does it matter? Where are you going?' — rapid-fire parental grilling
jokes.setup: David says he's busy tonight
jokes.punchline: Stevie's relentless interrogation parodies a concerned parent — each deflection is met with a new question
jokes.explanation: A fun escalation beat. The rapid-fire questioning is funny because it inverts the expected dynamic — Stevie has no real claim on David's time, making her interrogation absurdly controlling. Good character chemistry between them.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: On a scale of 1 to 10, how much danger is this outfit in?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'd say a solid 8.

jokes.text: David: 'I'm going to a bar to meet someone.' / Stevie: 'Who?!' / David: 'I don't know yet.'
jokes.setup: Stevie asks who David is meeting
jokes.punchline: He hasn't met them yet — the casual admission that he's trolling a bar for strangers, delivered with complete matter-of-factness
jokes.explanation: The economy of 'I don't know yet' as the punchline is excellent — it's the honest answer that makes the situation suddenly very clear and very funny. Good deadpan delivery implied.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, you can't go to a sketchy bar without me. Dangerous situations are literally my specialty.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Ohh... So she's going to a bar to get laid, then.' / David: correcting the terms with elaborate dignity
jokes.setup: Stevie casually summarizes what David is doing
jokes.punchline: David refuses the blunt framing: 'she is going to a bar to have her needs met by some lovely gentleman she's yet to meet, who is cute enough to sleep with, but not nearly mature or stable enough to marry'
jokes.explanation: David's elaborate euphemism for 'getting laid' is perfectly in character — his refusal of blunt language in favor of high-register circumlocution, while the content is entirely explicit. 'Cute enough to sleep with, but not nearly mature or stable enough to marry' is a quotable line with real character specificity. Good craft.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: If you're ever in a sketchy bar and someone tries to kidnap you, you have to go for the eyes first. Then you scream really loud so people know you're being kidnapped and not just, like, having a bad time.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's... oddly specific.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I've been kidnapped twice.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'I want that.' — immediately after David's elaborate description of his bar plans
jokes.setup: David has just finished describing his mission for the evening with elaborate dignity
jokes.punchline: Stevie's flat, three-word admission cuts through all of David's verbal gymnastics
jokes.explanation: Three words. Maximum economy. After David's elaborate paragraph of dignified circumlocution, Stevie cuts straight to the heart of it. The contrast between David's verbal elaborateness and Stevie's flat directness is the engine of their relationship, and this is a perfect expression of it. The economy score is 10 — there is literally no more efficient way to deliver this joke. Highly quotable, strong rewatch bonus.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, this is it. This is how democracy dies.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, they're both on the town council.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, and now we're living in a Mad Max film, but with more cardigan sweaters and a Costco membership.

jokes.text: David: 'Well, you can't come. 'Cause everyone will think we're together.' / Stevie: 'Okay, I think you're giving yourself a lot of credit.'
jokes.setup: David says Stevie can't come because people will think they're a couple
jokes.punchline: Stevie deflates his vanity: 'I think you're giving yourself a lot of credit.' / David: 'I was being generous.'
jokes.explanation: A sharp two-beat exchange. David's vanity is punctured by Stevie, and then his 'I was being generous' pivot is a great recovery — he acknowledges the self-deprecation while maintaining some perverse dignity. Their rapport shines here.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm worried that Roland and Jocelyn are going to control the entire town council.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: They already do. They control everything.

jokes.text: David: 'How diverse is the clientele at this local drinkery?' / Stevie: 'I would say very diverse.'
jokes.setup: David asks about the diversity of the bar's clientele
jokes.punchline: Stevie says 'very diverse' — in a way that clearly means it's not, setting up David's hopes for a disappointment
jokes.explanation: Stevie's 'very diverse' is a setup for the eventual reveal of what the bar is actually like. On rewatch you understand exactly what she means. Good economy and the irony is well-placed.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, Jocelyn suggested it. She said, 'You'd make a great town council member.' I mean, at first I thought she was just being nice, but then I realized she was clearly manipulating me into running.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, that's... that's not manipulation. That's a compliment.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, is it? Because to me it sounded like psychological warfare wrapped in a cashmere cardigan.

jokes.text: David: 'I can't remember what life was like before dating apps. I'm both excited and terrified for tonight.'
jokes.setup: David and Stevie prepare for a night out at a physical bar
jokes.punchline: David's admission that he's already forgotten how to meet people in person — and the equal measures of excitement and terror
jokes.explanation: Relatable observational humor delivered with David's particular brand of neurotic self-awareness. Not exceptional but functions well as character establishment.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, that's... that's wonderful. I'm very happy for you. Very happy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I mean, I was going to run, but you know, your timing is impeccable as always.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, no, I'm kidding. This is great. This is really great. You're going to be fantastic.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Probably better than I would have been anyway.

jokes.text: Stevie in the background: 'I'm in Room 7 in case you need me. See you later. This is gonna be so fun for us.'
jokes.setup: Stevie said she wasn't coming to the bar
jokes.punchline: She's clearly already planning on going — 'This is gonna be so fun for us' while claiming she'll be in her room
jokes.explanation: A nice button — Stevie's 'This is gonna be so fun for us' while claiming she'll be in her room gives away exactly what she plans to do. Her deadpan self-deception mirrors Alexis's solo-time hypocrisy in the parallel storyline.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You know, it's weird. The bar's been completely dead all night. Then David walked in and... nothing changed.

jokes.text: John tells the story about 'the rumor going around' that he's running for council — Moira immediately assumes it's the same rumor about HER
jokes.setup: John says there's a funny rumor going around about him
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately co-opts it: 'I heard that one — it's only because you converse with women easily, and you dress so well.' — she's describing HER reputation, not his
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed misdirection. Moira's 'I heard that one — it's only because you converse with women easily, and you dress so well' is clearly about a rumor about HIM being gay, but she applies it to her own situation so naturally that it takes a beat to land. The joke requires Moira's self-absorption and John's bewilderment to work — perfect character integration. 'If this continues, I'll have to call a neurologist' is the perfect button.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: 79 miles? That's like, what, a six-hour drive? By the time you get there, you'll hate them.

jokes.text: Moira: 'If this continues, I'll have to call a neurologist.'
jokes.setup: John is confused because Moira responded to his rumor as if it were her rumor
jokes.punchline: Moira diagnoses the confusion as a neurological issue — in HER — rather than recognizing her own self-absorption as the cause
jokes.explanation: Moira diagnosing HER potential neurological issue — when she's the source of the confusion — is a perfect expression of her narcissism. The line is quotable and very Moira. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Oh, 79 miles? That's basically next door. You should definitely make the drive. What's 79 miles? That's like, what, ten minutes?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's not ten minutes, Stevie.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I know it's not ten minutes, David. I was being sarcastic. But seriously, you should go. Love is worth the drive. You could make it a road trip. Stop for snacks, listen to some music, really enjoy the journey.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Trying to get council to focus on the simplest thing is like... wrangling monkeys!' / John: 'Well, I can wrangle monkeys. It's called running a business.'
jokes.setup: Moira is discouraging John from running by describing how chaotic council is
jokes.punchline: John's literal acceptance of the monkey metaphor — 'I can wrangle monkeys, it's called running a business' — and Moira's immediate pivot to use it against him
jokes.explanation: A nice bit of verbal judo — Moira sets up the monkey metaphor, John grabs it and turns it to his advantage, Moira immediately reclaims it ('Yes, you could, but you'd be wasting your talents'). The back-and-forth is well-written.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This is a nightmare. There's no one here.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, it's a Tuesday night at a small-town bar. What were you expecting?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't know, someone age-appropriate? Someone who doesn't have a reverse mortgage?

jokes.text: Moira: 'Exactly, it's beneath you.' — after John says it's a 'giant step down from anything I've done in the past'
jokes.setup: John is wavering on running for council, framing it as beneath him
jokes.punchline: Moira enthusiastically agrees that it's beneath him — while clearly maneuvering to take the seat herself
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony here is strong — we can see exactly what Moira is doing while John cannot. Her enthusiasm ('Exactly, it's beneath you!') while clearly wanting the seat for herself is classic Moira scheming. Rewatch bonus for the dramatic irony.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, the good news is someone woke up.

jokes.text: Alexis to Stevie: 'You're a strong woman who's alone a lot. Like, I know you dated David, but everyone's allowed a mistake, am I right?'
jokes.setup: Alexis opens a conversation with Stevie about being alone
jokes.punchline: She slips in a brutal aside about David ('everyone's allowed a mistake') without missing a beat — completely tactless
jokes.explanation: A sharp character joke that works on multiple levels: (1) Alexis's casual dismissal of her own brother as a 'mistake,' (2) her tactical use of it to build rapport with Stevie, (3) Stevie's response ('You sound like my conscience'). The economy is excellent — 'everyone's allowed a mistake' does enormous comedic work. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: We could shoot some pool.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not familiar with that game.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's billiards. You use a cue stick.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't take requests.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'I know everyone in this town, so for the most part, I would rather be here by myself than engage in meaningless conversation with people I don't care about.'
jokes.setup: Alexis asks why Stevie prefers being alone
jokes.punchline: Stevie's blunt, completely unironic explanation — which functions simultaneously as a description of her character and an inadvertent insult to Alexis, who is standing right there
jokes.explanation: A beautifully double-edged line — Stevie means it genuinely but it's also a description of her attitude toward Alexis's visit. The audience picks up the implication before Alexis does. Strong deadpan and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: They have these checkpoints in Johannesburg, right? And they're like... they're basically just like a Wendy's drive-thru, except instead of a Frosty, they give you a gun to the head.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Totally.' — after Stevie explains she prefers being alone to meaningless conversation
jokes.setup: Stevie has just described preferring solitude to meaningless conversation — while Alexis is actively engaging her in meaningless conversation
jokes.punchline: Alexis's breezy 'Totally.' — completely oblivious to the implication
jokes.explanation: One word. Maximum ironic impact. 'Totally' from Alexis — the person who cannot tolerate being alone for five minutes — is peak character comedy. The economy score is near-perfect.

jokes.text: Moira interrupts Jocelyn's council announcement to make it about herself — 'I certainly know how to command a crowd'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn announces she's running for council to the Jazzagals
jokes.punchline: Moira uses the conversation to pivot to her own potential candidacy, despite having told John she'd sooner poke her eyes out with hat pins
jokes.explanation: The comedy here is the callback — Moira who would 'sooner poke her eyes out with hat pins' is now clearly warming to the idea. Her pivot from 'I certainly know how to command a crowd' is a beautiful character moment that sets up the episode's central reversal.

jokes.text: Jocelyn: 'I'm currently running unopposed. So that kinda takes away the excitement of winning, you know, but still, I think it's gonna be a real hoot.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn announces her candidacy
jokes.punchline: Her immediate deflation of her own victory — admitting running unopposed 'takes away the excitement' but remaining cheerful about it
jokes.explanation: Jocelyn's honest, sunny self-deflation is endearing and funny. 'I think it's gonna be a real hoot' as the punchline on her political announcement is perfectly in character — she's not ambitious, she's just helpful. The joke also sets up Moira's competitive instincts being triggered.

jokes.text: Moira: 'So Jocelyn, good for you. Taking on a new little project.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn has just announced her council run
jokes.punchline: Moira's spectacularly condescending framing of a political campaign as a 'little project'
jokes.explanation: The word 'little' is doing all the work here. Moira's condescension is so beautifully calibrated — she congratulates Jocelyn while diminishing her in the same breath. 'Taking on a new little project' is pure Moira Rose. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Jocelyn: 'She's not interested in politics!' — cutting off Moira before she can talk herself into running publicly
jokes.setup: Moira is very visibly warming to the idea of running, despite having denied it
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn — who will soon be running against her — cuts her off with 'She's not interested in politics!' in a moment of gentle dramatic irony
jokes.explanation: Dramatic irony that will pay off when Moira officially enters the race. Jocelyn defending Moira from the perception of running while Moira is actively thinking about it is a good layered moment.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Although I can see why someone might think that. I certainly know how to command a crowd, and I've never been one to shy away from a good fight.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn just said Moira is not interested in politics
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately contradicts the denial by listing her political qualifications with obvious relish
jokes.explanation: Moira trying to deny interest in running while transparently auditioning for the role is perfectly played. 'I've never been one to shy away from a good fight' as a political qualification, delivered with theatrical pride, is quintessentially Moira.

jokes.text: Jocelyn: 'Yeah, a real election race could be fun!' — completely missing that Moira is about to challenge her
jokes.setup: Moira has been transparently auditioning to enter the race
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn enthusiastically encourages competition, not realizing the competition is the woman standing right next to her
jokes.explanation: Jocelyn's cheerful obliviousness about who the competition will be is a good sustained irony. Her warmth makes it funnier — she genuinely thinks competition is great news.

jokes.text: Moira: 'If Jocelyn can do it, anyone can.'
jokes.setup: Moira is encouraging people to run — while implying anyone could meet the low bar Jocelyn sets
jokes.punchline: 'If Jocelyn can do it, anyone can' — a compliment that is a devastating insult in thin disguise
jokes.explanation: One of the best lines in the episode. The economy is near-perfect — eight words that simultaneously encourage civic participation and utterly demolish Jocelyn's credentials. The structure is impeccable: it sounds like encouragement until the implication lands. Only Moira Rose could deliver this with genuine sincerity. Highly quotable, strong character integration, and it works even better if Jocelyn is standing right there. Justifiably scores above 8.

jokes.text: David to Alexis: 'On a scale of one to I'm-gonna-get-beat-up by-an-angry-local, where do we see this look fall?' / Alexis: 'Um, like, easy six.'
jokes.setup: David is about to go out to a local bar and is concerned about his look
jokes.punchline: The elaborate custom scale ('I'm-gonna-get-beat-up-by-an-angry-local') and Alexis's breezy 'easy six' — implying genuine danger without concern
jokes.explanation: A very funny bit — David's fish-out-of-water anxiety encoded in a novel scale, and Alexis's 'easy six' as cheerful confirmation that he will probably be beaten up. The specificity of the scale name is the craft. Alexis's unconcern is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Alexis's elaborately reasonable justification for why SHE should be at a sketchy bar meeting randoms, culminating in 'I'm the one who should go missing!'
jokes.setup: Alexis objects that David is going to a sketchy bar without her
jokes.punchline: 'I'm the one who should go missing!' — delivered as a legitimate grievance, framing potential abduction as something she's owed
jokes.explanation: Alexis's 'I'm the one who should go missing!' is a brilliantly dark, character-specific joke. It implies a history of dangerous and glamorous situations, delivered as a complaint about being left out. The absurdity of treating potential abduction as a personal entitlement is peak Alexis Rose. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Well, for one thing, this mask doesn't come off for another twenty minutes.' — why she can't go to the bar
jokes.setup: David invites Alexis to just come along to the bar
jokes.punchline: Alexis's first reason is that her face mask isn't done — a deeply trivial obstacle elevated to priority status
jokes.explanation: The visual of Alexis in a face mask being completely serious about it is a reliable physical comedy beat. Her priorities are perfectly Alexis — the face mask trumps missing out on the night.

jokes.text: David's duct-tape escape tip: 'Oh, and in case you wake up in a chair with your hands duct taped together, you can snap the duct tape by just raising your hands over your head, and then bringing them down really hard.'
jokes.setup: David is saying goodbye to Alexis before going to the bar to meet strangers
jokes.punchline: He offers a completely specific and practical kidnapping-survival tip as a casual goodbye
jokes.explanation: A standout joke. The specificity of the duct-tape escape instruction — as if David has researched this for exactly this situation — is brilliantly absurd. It implies an entire backstory (why does David know this? What has happened to this family?) while functioning as a perfect deadpan button on Alexis's 'I'm the one who should go missing' line. Very quotable, strong rewatch bonus. Justifiably scores 8+.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Thanks. Um... see you there?' / David: 'I'm not coming.'
jokes.setup: Alexis has just said she's staying home
jokes.punchline: She immediately flips to 'see you there?' — she was clearly planning on going the whole time. David mirrors her earlier line back at her.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully mirrored callback — David turns Stevie's 'I'm not coming' back on Alexis, while Alexis reveals she was never going to stay home. The structural symmetry of the callback is well-executed, and Alexis's 'see you there?' is perfectly casual.

jokes.text: John: 'Did you know Jocelyn is running?' / Moira: 'No, oh my God! This gets better and better.'
jokes.setup: John has just told Moira he's been talked out of running for council
jokes.punchline: Moira's enthusiasm upon hearing Jocelyn is running — 'This gets better and better!' — clearly because it gives her an opponent worth defeating
jokes.explanation: Moira's transparent competitive excitement is delicious — she's been maneuvering this whole time, and learning Jocelyn is running is the final ingredient. 'This gets better and better!' reveals everything. Good dramatic irony.

jokes.text: John and Moira bonding over the horror of Roland and Jocelyn having 'a virtual monopoly on this town'
jokes.setup: John mentions Jocelyn is running, and they both process this information
jokes.punchline: 'Is there a more frightening thought?' about Roland AND Jocelyn on council together — which immediately shifts into agreement that someone must challenge them
jokes.explanation: A functional comedy beat that advances the plot. The shared horror of the Schitt family's stranglehold on local politics is a reasonable joke but not sharply written. Works more as connective tissue than as a standalone comedic moment.

jokes.text: John: 'Is there a more frightening thought?' / Moira: 'Well, don't they already?' / John: 'Exactly, I think someone needs to challenge them!' / Moira: 'Right!' — the ping-pong negotiation
jokes.setup: John is building a case that someone should challenge the Schitts on council
jokes.punchline: Moira is enthusiastically agreeing with every argument John makes — for completely different reasons (he thinks she should encourage him; she's already decided she's running)
jokes.explanation: A sustained dramatic irony sequence — both characters are having completely different conversations. John thinks he's building a case for himself; Moira thinks she's receiving permission for herself. Well-structured and character-specific.

jokes.text: Moira: 'John, you have the family to think about. You're going to accomplish wonderfully big things. Town council would just hold you back. But something tells me it will not be... holding you back?'
jokes.setup: Moira has convinced John not to run, now pivoting to make the case for herself
jokes.punchline: The transparent self-insertion — 'something tells me it will not be... holding YOU back' — with the clear implication that she is the one for whom it will not be a setback
jokes.explanation: Moira's rhetorical sleight of hand — complimenting John out of the race while inserting herself — is beautifully written. The pause before 'holding you back?' is the moment the audience and John both realize what's happening.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Jocelyn practically begged me to take the bait!' — her justification for entering the race
jokes.setup: Moira announces she's running for council
jokes.punchline: 'Jocelyn practically begged me to take the bait' — an absurd reframing of the earlier scene where Jocelyn did no such thing
jokes.explanation: Moira's revisionist history — 'Jocelyn practically begged me' — is a perfect encapsulation of how she processes events. She has completely rewritten a scene we just watched. The character integration score is very high because this is entirely specific to Moira's psychology: she cannot be seen as pursuing anything; she must be reluctantly summoned to greatness. Very quotable.

jokes.text: 'Looks like it.' / 'Oh well, isn't that wonderful!' / 'Isn't that wonderful!'
jokes.setup: Moira reveals she's running for council — which she just talked John out of
jokes.punchline: John says 'isn't that wonderful' — twice — with the forced enthusiasm of a man processing that he just got maneuvered out of something he wanted
jokes.explanation: The doubled 'isn't that wonderful' is perfectly calibrated — once would be polite, twice reveals the processing delay. Eugene Levy's delivery of understated devastation is reliable gold. A good button on the scene's central joke.

jokes.text: John: 'No, listen as long as one of us is running, okay? That's the important thing. It doesn't matter which one of us is running. I thought it would be me, but then you talked me out of it! But that's okay!'
jokes.setup: Moira has announced she's running after John was talked out of it
jokes.punchline: John's escalating 'that's okay!' — a masterclass in dignified suppression of very real displeasure, accelerating through each clause
jokes.explanation: Eugene Levy's escalating 'that's okay!' is a performance comedy peak — the line reads increasingly like someone who is not okay at all. The structure of each added clause ('I thought it would be me, but then you talked me out of it! But that's okay!') is well-written escalation.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Please John, I'm going to need you every step of the way.' / John: 'And I'll be right behind you Moira, supporting you, because... you're the one running, and I'm not!'
jokes.setup: Moira asks John for his support as she runs for council
jokes.punchline: John's passive-aggressive 'because... you're the one running, and I'm not!' — perfectly calibrated resentment delivered through surface-level support
jokes.explanation: A great button on the John/Moira arc of the episode. His 'you're the one running, and I'm not!' is the purest expression of John's dignified, barely-suppressed exasperation. The 'and I'm not!' tag is the key — the unnecessary emphasis reveals the wound.

jokes.text: Bar scene: 'It's not usually this dead.' / Stevie: 'It's like everybody saw you coming, and left.'
jokes.setup: The bar is completely empty
jokes.punchline: Stevie's deadpan explanation: everyone left when they saw David coming
jokes.explanation: A good payoff on the 'very diverse clientele' setup. Stevie's deadpan 'It's like everybody saw you coming, and left' is perfectly timed and characterizes David's effect on the local population efficiently.

jokes.text: David's dating app match: '79 miles away.' / Stevie: 'Well, if you start walking now, you'll get there before breakfast.'
jokes.setup: David reveals he has a dating app match
jokes.punchline: The match is 79 miles away — and Stevie's suggestion to start walking to get there by breakfast
jokes.explanation: A clean observational joke about rural dating-app life. '79 miles away' is a perfect specific detail (not '80,' which would sound approximate), and 'start walking now, you'll get there before breakfast' is a well-structured punchline with good character voice for Stevie.

jokes.text: David: 'Alexis is supposed to be meeting us here. She said she wasn't coming, but this is a bar, and she is single, so.'
jokes.setup: David and Stevie are alone at the empty bar
jokes.punchline: David correctly predicts Alexis will show up despite saying she wouldn't — 'she said she wasn't coming, but this is a bar, and she is single' — a perfect character assessment
jokes.explanation: A callback that pays off Alexis's whole alone-time storyline. David's flat-affect character analysis — 'she said she wasn't coming, but this is a bar, and she is single, so' — is perfectly calibrated. The trailing 'so' does all the work.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Well, you should warn her that we are the only people here under sixty.'
jokes.setup: David mentions Alexis is coming to the bar to meet someone
jokes.punchline: Stevie's dry 'you should warn her that we are the only people here under sixty' — reframing the bar's 'diversity' as demographic disaster
jokes.explanation: A callback that pays off 'very diverse' from earlier. The understated reveal of what the bar's clientele actually looks like is perfectly Stevie. Good economy.

jokes.text: David and Stevie's silent, synchronized rejection: 'Nope.' / 'No!' / 'No, it didn't feel right.' / 'No, it didn't.'
jokes.setup: David and Stevie have apparently just rejected some social approach or interaction at the bar
jokes.punchline: Their perfectly synchronized mutual rejection, delivered with total alignment — 'at least we tried'
jokes.explanation: A charming reaction beat — we don't see what they rejected, making it funnier. Their perfect alignment ('No, it didn't feel right.' 'No, it didn't.') and immediate consolation ('at least we tried') is a lovely bit of character chemistry.

jokes.text: David noticing the man is 'no longer sleeping under the pool table'
jokes.setup: David and Stevie are at the largely empty bar
jokes.punchline: David's casual observation that the man previously sleeping under the pool table has vacated — treated as an unremarkable detail that is, in fact, quite remarkable
jokes.explanation: A beautiful visual gag inferred from dialogue — we now understand the bar is the kind of place where people sleep under pool tables, delivered as a matter-of-fact observation. The deadpan framing (it's just a detail, not a point of alarm) is the joke.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Well, I'm surprisingly good at pool.' — as an explanation of why the man moved from under the table
jokes.setup: David notes the man is no longer under the pool table
jokes.punchline: Stevie's explanation — 'Well, I'm surprisingly good at pool' — implying she's been playing and the balls kept landing on him. Delivered utterly straight.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The economy is near-perfect — six words that (1) confirm there was a man sleeping under the pool table, (2) imply Stevie has been aggressively playing pool regardless, (3) suggest she's been hitting him with balls and he left, and (4) are delivered with complete deadpan self-satisfaction. The 'surprisingly' is the key word — she's surprised by her own competence, which makes the implied aggression funnier. Very quotable, strong rewatch bonus. Justifiably 8+.

jokes.text: David: 'Wait a second, is this like a reverse hustle, where you say you're good, but you're actually terrible?' / Stevie: 'I don't know. Do you wanna break?' / David: 'Um, from what?' / Stevie: 'Oh, this is gonna be fun.'
jokes.setup: David questions whether Stevie is hustling him
jokes.punchline: Stevie's 'Do you wanna break?' (pool term) / David's 'Um, from what?' (he doesn't know what 'break' means in pool) / Stevie's 'Oh, this is gonna be fun' — the hustle has already begun
jokes.explanation: A perfectly structured three-beat joke. Stevie's 'Do you wanna break?' tests David's pool knowledge; his 'Um, from what?' confirms he has none; her 'Oh, this is gonna be fun' is the predatory punchline of someone who has found their mark. The callback to the pool-table-sleeper makes it richer. Strong structure and character integration.

jokes.text: David: 'Don't, David.' / 'I have nowhere else to put stuff.' — Alexis has shown up at the bar and is trying to stuff her things under the pool table where David is playing
jokes.setup: David and Stevie are at the bar after Alexis said she wasn't coming
jokes.punchline: Alexis has arrived (as predicted) and is immediately making David's life difficult by cramming her belongings under the pool table — echoing the motel room dispute
jokes.explanation: A double callback — Alexis showing up (paying off David's prediction) AND cramming her things under the pool table (echoing the 'temporary storage' under David's bed from the opening). The parallel structure is well-crafted. 'I have nowhere else to put stuff' is Alexis in a nutshell.

jokes.text: 'Don't, David. I have nowhere else to put stuff.'
jokes.setup: Presumably David has returned and is confronting Alexis again about her things under his bed
jokes.punchline: Alexis preemptively defends the clutter with the perfectly helpless 'I have nowhere else to put stuff' — as if this ends the argument
jokes.explanation: A callback to the opening room scene. The joke is in Alexis's preemptive defense — she already knows what David is going to say — and the complete lack of guilt in 'I have nowhere else to put stuff.' Functional callback but not escalated beyond the original.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'It's just a checkpoint. Okay, I've been through tons of these in Johannesburg. It's like a drive-thru, except everybody has a gun.'
jokes.setup: There is apparently a checkpoint on the way to or from the bar
jokes.punchline: Alexis's casual comparison of the checkpoint to a drive-thru 'except everybody has a gun' — delivered as reassurance, treating armed checkpoints as a normal life experience
jokes.explanation: A standout Alexis joke. 'It's like a drive-thru, except everybody has a gun' is a perfectly constructed dark joke — the mundane frame ('drive-thru') against the alarming content ('everybody has a gun'), delivered as reassurance. Only Alexis Rose would treat armed checkpoints as a childhood normalcy. Very quotable, high character specificity. Justifiably scores 8+.

jokes.text: David: 'When were you in Johannesburg?' / Alexis: 'I remember I'd just got my braces off, so...' / David: 'You were 14, in South Africa?'
jokes.setup: Alexis mentions her experience with armed checkpoints in Johannesburg
jokes.punchline: She dates it by when she got her braces off — she was 14 in a conflict zone — David's horrified arithmetic is the final punchline
jokes.explanation: A perfect escalation of the previous joke. Alexis dating her Johannesburg trip by orthodontic milestones ('I'd just got my braces off, so...') is a beautifully mundane anchor for a wildly abnormal experience. David's horrified 'You were 14, in South Africa?' captures the audience's reaction. The juxtaposition of braces and armed checkpoints is the entire joke, and it works because it tells us everything about Alexis's childhood.

tmdb_overview: Johnny considers running for town council. David and Stevie look for love. Alexis copes with being single.
insight: Character-driven episode lands 48 jokes in 41 minutes, balancing awkward family moments with political campaign chaos.
